data.history: The Al Midfa family originated in Trucial Oman but moved to Sharjah in 1660, where they became wealthy traders in foodstuffs, perfumes and later pearling. By the early 20th century, they had established themselves as prominent merchants and intellectuals in Sharjah. Ibrahim Bin Mohammed Al Midfa, born in 1909, became a significant cultural and intellectual figure, building a house in central Sharjah using imported teak in 1927, demonstrating the family's considerable wealth. He launched Sharjah's first newspaper, Oman, in 1927, becoming the emirate's first journalist and establishing regular gatherings of writers, poets and political activists at his majlis. The family's influence extended beyond media - Ibrahim also established Sharjah's first postal service and published a second periodical called Sawt Al Asafeer (the Sound of Birds). The Al Midfa house, with its unique circular barjeel (windtower) decorated with tiles imported from India, became a cultural center and today forms part of the Chedi Al Bait Hotel development, with two of Ibrahim's brothers' houses also incorporated into the hotel structure.
data.description: A prominent merchant family in Sharjah since 1660, known for their wealth from trading and pearling, and for producing Ibrahim Bin Mohammed Al Midfa, Sharjah's first journalist and cultural intellectual.
data.originStory: The Al Midfa family originated in Trucial Oman but relocated to Sharjah in 1660, where they established themselves as successful traders dealing in foodstuffs, perfumes and later pearling operations.
data.modernStatus: The family's historical legacy is preserved through the Bait Al Midfa, which is now part of the Chedi Al Bait Hotel development in Sharjah, where items of Al Midfa's possessions are displayed in museum cabinets.

data.history: The research provides information about three distinct Al Ham/Bin Ham families but does not establish clear genealogical connections between them. Sheikh Ahmed bin Hamed al Hamed (1929-2012) was an Emirati royal and politician who served as the UAE's first Minister of Information and Tourism. He was described as a maternal cousin to Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the UAE's founding president, and uncle to Sheikh Khalifa, indicating close ties to the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi. Sheikh Hamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, son of Sheikh Zayed, serves as managing director of Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and holds senior positions in Abu Dhabi's government. The Bin Ham Group appears as a separate business entity, with Sheikh Mohammed bin Musallam bin Ham Al-Ameri serving as its Deputy Chairman and tracing his lineage through Sheikh Musallam bin Salem bin Ham Al Ameri and Sheikh Salem bin Ham, who reportedly played formative roles in the early years of the UAE.

data.modernStatus: The families hold significant positions in UAE government and business. Sheikh Hamed bin Zayed serves as managing director of Abu Dhabi Investment Authority and chairman of Etihad Airways. The Bin Ham Group operates as a national economic group with Sheikh Mohammed bin Musallam as Deputy Chairman.

data.history: The Al Mehairi (also Al Mheiri or Al Muhairi) are documented as the Al Bu Muhair tribe, one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation that originated from the Najd region of Central Arabia. According to historical records, the Al Bu Muhair were closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi but settled throughout the western coastal areas of the Emirates. In 1841, approximately 500 members of the Al Bu Muhair left Dubai due to dissatisfaction with the rule of Maktoum bin Butti, who had led the Al Bu Falasah from Abu Dhabi to establish Dubai in 1833, and subsequently settled in Sharjah. This movement, combined with a plague outbreak in Dubai, led to the temporary abandonment of Dubai and its virtual desertion. Another contingent of the tribe left Dubai for Sharjah in 1852, indicating continued political tensions. By the turn of the 20th century, the Al Bu Muhair had established significant settlements across the UAE coastal regions, with 500 members in Abu Dhabi, another 500 in Al Bateen, 400 houses in Dubai, and substantial populations in Sharjah (60 houses), Ajman (80 houses), Umm Al Quwain (30 houses), and Ras Al Khaimah (120-200 houses according to different sources). The Al Bu Muhair first settled in Ras Al Khaimah from Abu Dhabi with the establishment of Al Qawasim rule in the 1750s. In modern times, the family has achieved prominence in various sectors including politics, business, and sports, with notable members serving in high government positions and leading major enterprises.
data.description: The Al Mehairi (Al Bu Muhair) are a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, historically settled throughout the UAE's western coastal areas with significant populations in Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, and the northern emirates. They have achieved modern prominence in government, business, and sports.
data.originStory: The Al Bu Muhair descended from the Bani Yas tribal confederation, which originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, tracing their lineage to Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a along with other Bani Yas branches.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mehairi family has achieved significant prominence in the UAE, with members holding high government positions including Mariam Almheiri as head of the International Affairs Office at the Presidential Court and former Minister of Climate Change and Environment, and Mohamed Al Mehairi serving as Chairman of Depa Limited Board. Family members are also involved in major business enterprises and sports.

King Khalid bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1913-1982) was the fifth son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz and ruled from 1975 until his death. His mother, Al Jawhara bint Musaed, was from the Al Jiluwi clan, and he had one full brother, Muhammad. During his reign, Saudi Arabia experienced massive development due to increased oil revenues, with income rising from $40 billion in 1977 to $90 billion in 1980. Other individuals in the research include Crown Prince Sabah Al-Khalid Al-Sabah of Kuwait (born 1953), who served as Foreign Minister and Prime Minister before his appointment as Crown Prince in 2024, and Sheikh Khaled bin Mohamed Al Nahyan of the UAE (born 1982), appointed Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi in 2023. Like other Bedouin Emiratis, the Al Ghafli have historically lived nomadically in the desert, adapting to harsh conditions by moving with their families and animals in search of water and grazing areas. While modernization and urbanization have brought changes to Emirati society, many Bedouin Emiratis, including those from Al Ghafli lineage, continue to maintain ties to their nomadic heritage even as they settle into more sedentary lifestyles in urban areas.

data.history: The Dubai International Financial Centre (DIFC) was established in 2004 as a special economic zone covering 110 hectares, created as a financial hub for companies operating throughout the Middle East, Africa, and South Asia (MEASA) markets. By 2022, DIFC had experienced significant growth with a 19% annual increase in non-financial firms, bringing the total number of international companies, family businesses, and corporate service providers to over 3,000. The zone operates as an independent jurisdiction under the UAE Constitution with its own civil and commercial laws distinct from the wider UAE, using English law as its framework and operating its own court system separate from Dubai's legal system. DIFC offers companies 100% ownership without local partners and a 50-year guarantee of zero taxes on corporate income and profits. The centre houses the Dubai Financial Services Authority as its regulator and includes NASDAQ Dubai (formerly Dubai International Financial Exchange, established in 2005), along with various financial institutions, wealth funds, retail spaces, and hotels including the Ritz-Carlton, Waldorf Astoria, and Four Seasons.

The Yusuf family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arabian Peninsula and Indian Ocean region, with no single origin or connection between the various branches. In Mumbai, the Yusuf family traces to Kutchi Memon origins in modern Gujarat, with Haji Hasham founding the Bombay Steam Navigation Company as the first Indian-owned shipping enterprise. His son Haji Ismail Yusuf (c.1870-1915) expanded the business, followed by Sir Mohamed Yusuf (1876-1965), who was knighted in 1914 and established major educational institutions including Training Ship Rahaman (1910) and Ismail Yusuf College (1923). The family became one of Mumbai's ten largest private landowners through strategic land acquisitions. In Bahrain, the Kanoo branch began when Haji Yusuf bin Ahmed Kanoo established a trading business in 1890, evolving from his father's operations into what became the YBA Kanoo Group, now one of the largest family-owned multinationals in the Middle East with operations across shipping, travel, industrial services, and real estate. The various Yusuf families demonstrate the broader pattern of merchant communities establishing trading empires across the Indian Ocean commercial networks.
data.description: Multiple distinct Yusuf family lineages including Kutchi Memon merchants in Mumbai who founded major shipping and educational enterprises, and Bahraini traders who built the YBA Kanoo Group conglomerate.
data.originStory: The Mumbai Yusuf family originated from Kutchi Memon communities in modern Gujarat, while the Bahraini Kanoo branch developed from local trading activities established in 1890.
data.modernStatus: The Mumbai Yusuf family remains among the city's largest private landowners with major educational institutions and charitable foundations. The YBA Kanoo Group operates as a major multinational conglomerate across the Gulf with 4,000+ employees and presence in multiple countries.

data.history: The Al-Khelaifi family name appears in various Arabic transcriptions (Al-Khelaifi and Al-Khulaifi) and is found among notable figures across Qatar and the broader Gulf region. The most prominent member is Nasser Al-Khelaifi, born in 1973 in Qatar, who became a major business executive and sports figure. The family has established significant presence in Qatar's business and sports sectors, with members active in swimming, chess, diplomacy, and human rights advocacy. The name's etymology suggests 'my successor' in Arabic, though specific tribal origins or migration patterns are not documented in available sources. The family's modern prominence emerged through individual achievements rather than traditional tribal or merchant networks, representing a newer generation of Gulf business families who gained influence through professional and commercial success rather than hereditary status.

data.history: The Al Hogail family appears in the provided research primarily through Majid Al-Hogail, who serves as Saudi Arabia's Minister of Housing. He was appointed to this position in July 2015, taking leadership of a ministry that was established in 2011 by royal decree to address housing issues in the Kingdom. Under his leadership, the Ministry has launched various programs including the Sakani program, which aims to provide housing products to Saudi citizens and increase home ownership to 70% by 2030. In 2018, Minister Al-Hogail demonstrated the ministry's commitment to innovation by handing over the first house built in 48 hours using 3D-printed building technology to its owner. The housing sector investment under his guidance was expected to reach $21.33 billion in 2019 to provide affordable housing units.

data.history: The Al Suwayed family appears to have branches across the Gulf region, with documented members in both Kuwait and Saudi Arabia involved in professional football. The Kuwaiti branch includes Yussef Faraj Al-Suwayed, born in 1958, who became a prominent midfielder for both Kazma Sporting Club and the Kuwait national team, representing his country in the 1982 FIFA World Cup with 61 international caps. The Saudi branch includes Ibrahim Suwayed Mahboub Al-Shahrani, born in 1974, who played for Al-Ahli and the Saudi national team, participating in both the 1998 and 2002 FIFA World Cups. The family's connection to football excellence spans two generations and two Gulf nations, suggesting either migration between Kuwait and Saudi Arabia or separate branches of the same lineage. Both individuals achieved significant recognition in Gulf football during the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

data.history: The Al Sheibani family traces its lineage to the historic Banu Shayban tribe, an ancient Arab tribal confederation with significant historical presence across the Middle East and Central Asia. The most documented branch of this lineage appears in modern Syria, where Asaad Hassan al-Shaibani was born in 1987 in the eastern countryside of al-Hasakah Governorate to a family belonging to the Banu Shayban tribe, before moving with his family to Damascus. The family's historical connections extend to maritime trade and diplomacy, as evidenced by Ahmad bin Na'aman Al Ka'abi's relationship with the Al Shaibani family through marriage - his daughter Maryam married Nasser bin Saleh bin Suleiman Al Shaibani, and Ahmad bin Na'aman's estate, including his historic logbook from the first Arab diplomatic mission to the United States in 1840, was inherited by Abdulamir bin Ali bin Saleh Al-Sheibani. This inheritance pattern suggests the Al Sheibani family maintained significant social standing and connections to important diplomatic and commercial networks in the Gulf region, particularly around Oman and the broader Indian Ocean trade routes during the 19th century.
data.description: The Al Sheibani family is an Arab family tracing its lineage to the Banu Shayban tribe, with documented presence in Syria and historical connections to Gulf maritime trade and diplomacy through the 19th-century inheritance of significant diplomatic artifacts.
data.originStory: The family originates from the Banu Shayban tribe, with the Syrian branch documented in the eastern countryside of al-Hasakah Governorate before relocating to Damascus.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved significant political prominence in modern Syria, with Asaad Hassan al-Shaibani serving as Syria's Minister of Foreign Affairs and Expatriates since December 2024, playing a key role in the country's post-Assad government and international diplomatic relations.

data.history: The Al Hubail (أل حبيل) family traces their roots to the Baharna, an ethnoreligious group of Shia Muslim Arabs indigenous to the historical region of Bahrain. According to research, the Baharna are regarded by some scholars as the original inhabitants of Eastern Arabia, inhabiting the region before the arrival of the Banu Utbah in the 18th century from which the Bahraini royal family descends. The Al Hubail name appears to be directly connected to Abdulhadi Abdulla Hubail al-Khawaja, indicating 'Hubail' as a family lineage name within the broader Al-Khawaja family structure. The family's modern prominence emerged through political activism and sports, with members becoming notable figures in Bahrain's pro-democracy movement and football. During the 2011 Bahraini uprising, multiple Al Hubail family members were targeted by authorities for their participation in anti-government protests, reflecting the family's alignment with the broader Bahrana community's political struggles for democratic representation and human rights.

data.history: The Al Saffar name appears across different historical periods and regions with varying origins. The earliest historical reference is to Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar (840-879), a Persian coppersmith from Karnin (east of Zaranj, west of Bost in present-day Afghanistan) who founded the Saffarid dynasty. The name 'Saffar' derives from the Arabic/Persian word for coppersmith, reflecting his original profession before becoming a military leader and ruler of much of eastern Greater Iran. In modern times, the Al Saffar name appears prominently in the Arabian Gulf region, particularly among Shi'a communities in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, notably in Qatif where Sheikh Hassan al-Saffar (born 1958) has emerged as a leading Shi'a scholar and reformist. However, the research provides no direct genealogical connection between the medieval Persian Saffarids and the modern Gulf Al Saffar families. The name also appears in modern Iraq with figures like Ammar al-Saffar, a Deputy Health Minister who was kidnapped in 2006, though again no clear family connections are established in the available sources.
data.description: Al Saffar is a name with historical significance spanning from medieval Persian dynasties to modern Gulf Shi'a communities, with the most prominent contemporary figure being Sheikh Hassan al-Saffar, a leading Shi'a scholar from Qatif, Saudi Arabia.

data.modernStatus: The Al Saffar name is associated with prominent Shi'a religious leadership in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, with Sheikh Hassan al-Saffar serving as a key figure in Shi'a-Sunni dialogue and reform movements

data.history: The Al Sindi family appears to have historical roots tracing back to the Abbasid period, with Al-Sindi ibn Shahak serving as a prominent 8th-century general and administrator under multiple Abbasid caliphs including al-Mansur, Harun ar-Rashid and al-Amin. He served as governor of Syria, Damascus and Baghdad, and was described as 'apparently a former slaver from Punjab who had risen to high status.' His family continued to serve the Abbasid caliphate for multiple decades, with his son Ibrahim ibn al-Sindi becoming a noted polymath and his grandson Kushajim (c. 902-970) achieving fame as a court poet. In the modern era, the Al Sindi name gained international prominence through Dr. Hayat Sindi, born in Mecca in 1967, who became a pioneering Saudi biotechnologist and the first Saudi woman to obtain a PhD in Biotechnology from Cambridge University in 2001. Dr. Sindi has served as a UNESCO Goodwill Ambassador, founded multiple companies including Diagnostics for All, and became one of the first women appointed to Saudi Arabia's Consultative Council in 2013.
data.description: A family with historical Abbasid administrative roots and modern prominence through biotechnology pioneer Dr. Hayat Sindi, who became the first Saudi woman to earn a PhD in biotechnology from Cambridge.

data.modernStatus: The family has achieved international recognition through Dr. Hayat Sindi's pioneering work in biotechnology and medical science, with her serving on Saudi Arabia's Shura Council and various UN bodies. She has founded multiple companies and been recognized as one of the most influential Arab women globally.

data.history: The Al Tajer family is a branch of the historic Al Faiz family of Karbala, Iraq, one of the oldest Alid families in the city. They descended from Ali 'al-Tajir' al-Faizi, who was given the epithet 'al-Tajir' (the merchant) due to his frequent travels to East Asia for trade. The family has served in the holy Husayn and Abbas shrines in Karbala throughout their history. The Al Tajer family owns farms known as Umm al-Sudan in mahalat Al Faiz (now known as al-Hayabi), which were endowed by Hassan al-Tajir to his descendants in 1680. As part of the broader Al Faiz lineage, they claim descent from Ibrahim al-Mujab, the 9th-century grandson of the seventh Shi'ite Imam Musa al-Kadhim, through the patriarchal line of Muhammad Abu al-Faiz. The family has maintained their presence in Karbala for over a millennium, with their ancestors having held positions of religious and administrative authority in the city.
data.description: Al Tajer is a branch of the Al Faiz family of Karbala, Iraq, descended from Ali 'al-Tajir' al-Faizi who was known for his merchant activities in East Asia. The family has historically served in Karbala's holy shrines and owns hereditary agricultural lands.
data.originStory: The Al Tajer family originated as a branch of the Al Faiz family in Karbala, taking their name from Ali 'al-Tajir' al-Faizi, who earned his epithet through extensive trading expeditions to East Asia. Through the Al Faiz lineage, they trace their ancestry to Ibrahim al-Mujab, who settled in Karbala in 861 CE after al-Mutawakkil's death, and ultimately claim descent from the seventh Shi'ite Imam Musa al-Kadhim through his son Muhammad al-Abid.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains their presence in Iraq, with members involved in artistic and cultural pursuits. They continue to be recognized as part of the historic Alid families of Karbala.
data.nameEtymology: Al Tajer derives from the Arabic word 'tajir' meaning 'merchant', referencing their ancestor Ali al-Faizi's extensive trading activities, particularly his frequent commercial travels to East Asia.

data.history: The Bin Khalid family name appears across multiple Gulf ruling families and prominent lineages, but the research reveals distinct family lines rather than a single unified origin. The most prominent historical figure is King Khalid bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1913-1982), fifth son of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz, who ruled Saudi Arabia from 1975-1982. His mother Al Jawhara bint Musaed was from the Al Jiluwi clan, making him part of the broader Al Saud royal family network. In the UAE, Sheikh Khalid bin Mohamed Al Nahyan represents the Al Nahyan ruling family of Abu Dhabi, appointed Crown Prince in 2023. Qatar's leadership includes Sheikh Khalid bin Khalifa Al Thani, who served as Prime Minister from 2020-2023, representing the Al Thani ruling family. The contemporary Saudi royal family includes Prince Khalid bin Salman Al Saud, current Defense Minister and son of King Salman. Each of these represents distinct family lineages within their respective ruling houses rather than branches of a common 'Bin Khalid' origin.
data.description: Multiple distinct family lines bearing the Bin Khalid name across Gulf ruling families, most notably within the Al Saud, Al Nahyan, and Al Thani dynasties, rather than a single unified family origin.
data.modernStatus: Various Bin Khalid family members hold prominent positions across Gulf states: Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi (UAE), former Prime Minister of Qatar, Defense Minister of Saudi Arabia, and diplomatic positions including ambassadorships to the UK and Germany.

data.history: The Al Waberi family name appears to be connected to the Waberi district of Mogadishu, Somalia, one of eighteen administrative districts of the capital city. Mogadishu has been traditionally inhabited by four main clans: the Moorshe, Iskashato, DhabarWeyne, and the Bandawow, with the Moorshe regarded as the oldest group and considered a sub-clan of the Ajuran who established the powerful medieval Ajuran Sultanate. The city has a complex ethnic composition including indigenous Somali clans and the Benadiris (also called 'Ad'ad or Cadcad), a minority group produced by centuries of intermarriage between various ethnic groups including Somalis, Arabs, Persians, and Bantus, who mainly inhabit the oldest districts of Mogadishu. During the colonial period and afterward, the city's cosmopolitan nature was further enhanced by various immigrant communities, and following improved security in 2012, many Somali expatriates from the diaspora began returning to participate in post-conflict reconstruction.
data.description: Al Waberi appears to be associated with the Waberi district of Mogadishu, Somalia, within the complex multi-ethnic composition of the historic coastal city.

Yahya bin Mahfooth Al Manthari was appointed as the first Minister of Higher Education in 1994 when the ministry was established, taking over responsibilities that had previously been handled by the Directorate General of Scholarship and Foreign Relations under the Ministry of Education. The ministry was created to supervise higher education institutes and develop higher education policies in the Sultanate. data.history: The Al Quraini family traces its origins to the village of Al-Qurain in northern Al-Ahsa, Eastern Arabia. The most extensively documented figure is Ahmad bin Muhammad al-Muhsini (1744-1831), whose full lineage is recorded as: Jamal al-Din Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Muhsin bin Ali bin Muhammad bin Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hussein bin Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Khamis bin Saif, Al-Rab'i Al-Ahsa'i Al-Quraini. His father Muhammad resided in Al-Qurain village, establishing the family's connection to this location. Ahmad was born in Medina in 1744 during his father's travels to Hejaz, but was raised in Al-Ahsa under his father's care. He became the first in his family to be called 'Al-Muhsini,' a name derived from his grandfather's first name, though the family retained the Al-Quraini designation referencing their Al-Qurain origins. The family appears to have been established as religious scholars and jurists, with Ahmad receiving his initial education from his father before studying under Hussein Al-Asfoor in Al-Ahsa. In modern times, the family name continues with figures like Ahmed Nasser Abdullah Al-Quraini, born in 1991, who represents Oman as an international footballer, suggesting the family's presence has extended beyond their original Al-Ahsa base to include Oman.
data.description: Al Quraini is a family originating from the village of Al-Qurain in northern Al-Ahsa, Eastern Arabia, historically known for producing Islamic scholars and jurists, with documented lineage extending to modern times in both Saudi Arabia and Oman.
data.originStory: The family originates from the village of Al-Qurain in the northern region of Al-Ahsa, Eastern Arabia. The name Al-Quraini directly derives from this geographical origin, as confirmed by the detailed genealogical record of Ahmad al-Muhsini whose lineage specifically identifies him as 'Al-Quraini' in reference to the family's connection to Al-Qurain village.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains presence in multiple Gulf countries, with documented members including contemporary figures in Oman such as footballer Ahmed Nasser Abdullah Al-Quraini who represents the national team and plays professionally in the Omani league system.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Quraini derives from the village of Al-Qurain in northern Al-Ahsa, Eastern Arabia, where the family historically resided. The name directly references their geographical origin.

data.history: The Al Mazeedi family traces their lineage to the Banu Asad tribe, an ancient Arab tribe descended from Asad ibn Khuzayma. The Banu Asad are Adnanite Arabs who migrated to Iraq in the 7th century, settling near the banks of the Euphrates river near Kufa and Karbala, as well as in Basra and Ahvaz. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, particularly in the Battle of Karbala in 680 CE, where many Banu Asad members died alongside Husayn ibn Ali, and they later had the honor of burying the martyred bodies. In 998 CE, Ali ben Mazyad, leader of the Banu Asad tribe, established the virtually independent Mazyadid state in the Kufa area of Iraq, which lasted for a century and a half and founded the city of Hillah in 1101, which became their capital. The Al Mazeedi family name derives from this Mazyadid dynasty, with their tribal name being Al-Mazyad or Banu Asad, and their surnames including Assadi, Al-Assadi, or Al-Mazeedi. Members of the Al Mazeedi family later migrated from Iraq to various regions, including Kuwait, Yemen (Hadhramaut region), Oman, and even India (Karnataka state), with some settling in these areas a few hundred years ago.
data.description: The Al Mazeedi family are descendants of the Banu Asad tribe who established the medieval Mazyadid emirate in Iraq and later spread across the Gulf region, with notable members contributing to Kuwait's constitutional development.
data.originStory: The Al Mazeedi family originates from the Banu Asad tribe, descended from Asad ibn Khuzayma, tracing their lineage back through Mudar to Adnan and ultimately to Ishmael and Abraham. The family name derives from the Mazyadid dynasty established by Ali ben Mazyad in 998 CE in the Kufa area of Iraq.
data.modernStatus: The Al Mazeedi family includes both Shia and Sunni branches across the Gulf region. In Kuwait, Mansour Moosa Al-Mazeedi played an important role in developing Kuwait's Constitution issued on January 29, 1963 as part of the Founding Parliament.
data.nameEtymology: The name Al-Mazeedi derives from the Mazyadid dynasty, named after Mazyad ben Mikhled al Sadaqa, a scholar, hadith narrator and chemist who was the originator of the Mazyadid name in the Banu Asad tribe.

data.history: The Al Nimr family is a Saudi Arabian Shia family from Al-Awamiyah in the Eastern Province. The most prominent member was Ayatollah Sheikh Nimr Baqir al-Nimr (1959-2016), who became a significant religious and political figure among Saudi Shia Muslims. Sheikh Nimr began his religious studies in Al-Awamiyah before moving to Iran in 1980 to complete his education at the Al-Qaim seminary in Tehran, studying primarily under Ayatollah Ali-Akbar al-Modarresi. After the seminary was closed by Iranian authorities, he moved to Damascus, Syria, where he initially followed Grand Ayatollah Muhammad al-Shirazi and later Grand Ayatollah al-Modarresi. By 2008, Sheikh Nimr had become independent of the two main political groups in the Eastern Province Shia community and served as the leader of Friday prayers in Al-Awamiyah. His criticism of Saudi government policies and advocacy for Shia rights led to multiple arrests, culminating in his execution on January 2, 2016, along with 46 others, which sparked international condemnation and protests across the region.
data.description: A Saudi Shia family from Al-Awamiyah in the Eastern Province, most notably represented by executed cleric Sheikh Nimr al-Nimr. The family has faced significant persecution from Saudi authorities, with multiple members arrested, killed, or executed for political activism.
data.modernStatus: The family remains under severe persecution by Saudi authorities. Following Sheikh Nimr's execution in 2016, two cousins (Miqdad and Mohammad al-Nimr) were killed during a 2017 security forces raid. His nephew Ali Mohammed Baqir al-Nimr was sentenced to death as a minor but was eventually released in 2021. The family continues to be associated with Shia rights activism in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province.

data.history: The Al Rumhi family is documented in modern Omani government and energy sector leadership. Dr. Mohammed bin Hamad Al Rumhi served as Oman's Minister of Energy and Minerals from December 16, 1997 until 2022, making him one of the longest-serving ministers in this critical portfolio. During his tenure, he oversaw major developments in Oman's oil and gas sector, including serving as Chairman of the Board of Directors of Petroleum Development Oman. In 2004, Mohammed Al Rumhi, in his capacity as Oman's oil and gas minister and chairman of Qalhat LNG, signed a significant long-term LNG supply agreement with Japan's Mitsubishi Corporation for the third LNG train at the Qalhat complex. His leadership spanned a crucial period of Oman's energy sector expansion and diversification. He was succeeded by Salim bin Nasser bin Said Al Aufi as Minister of Energy and Minerals on June 16, 2022.

data.history: The Al Khozama family appears to be a prominent Saudi business family involved in hospitality and real estate management. Al-Khozama Management Company emerged as a significant player in Saudi Arabia's luxury hospitality sector, taking over management of the prestigious Al-Faisaliah Hotel from Rosewood Hotels & Resorts in April 2015, ending a 20-year partnership. The company demonstrated its business acumen by successfully transitioning the hotel to new management and later facilitating its transfer to the Hong Kong-based Mandarin Oriental Hotel Group in November 2020. The Al-Khozama Management Company's involvement in managing one of Riyadh's most prestigious hotels, known for hosting Saudi royal family weddings and serving the business elite, indicates their established position within Saudi Arabia's upper echelons of business and society. Their role in the hospitality sector appears to be part of Saudi Arabia's broader economic diversification efforts, particularly in the tourism and business services sectors that align with Vision 2030 initiatives.

data.history: The Al Shihri family name denotes membership in Bani Shehr, an Arabic tribal confederation. The family gained international prominence through the activities of two members detained at Guantanamo Bay: Sa'id Ali Jabir Al Khathim Al Shihri and Yussef Mohammed Mubarak al-Shihri. Sa'id Ali al-Shihri was captured at the Pakistan-Afghanistan border in December 2001 while allegedly involved in financing fighters' travel to Afghanistan. He was among the first detainees at Guantanamo Bay, arriving on January 21, 2002, and was held for almost six years before repatriation to Saudi custody on November 9, 2007. His younger relative Yussef al-Shihri was captured at age 16 near Kunduz with his older cousin as part of a group of 120 soldiers and was also transferred to Guantanamo on January 16, 2002. After their release through Saudi rehabilitation programs, both became involved with militant activities. Sa'id Ali co-founded Al Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula (AQAP) in January 2009, appearing in threatening YouTube videos as the organization's second-in-command. He was eventually killed in a U.S. drone strike in 2013 after surviving a previous 2012 attack that cost him his right eye, right ear, and part of his skull. Yussef was killed in a shootout with Saudi police on October 18, 2009, while apparently preparing a suicide attack. The family's involvement in these activities led to multiple relatives being named on Saudi Arabia's most wanted terrorist list and imprisoned on suspicion of terrorism, including brothers Faisal and Mustafa, and cousin Abdul Ghani Al-Shehri.

data.modernStatus: The family became internationally known through terrorist activities of former Guantanamo detainees Sa'id Ali and Yussef al-Shihri, with multiple family members subsequently named on Saudi most wanted lists and imprisoned on terrorism charges.

data.history: The Al Sahli (Arabic: الساهلي) family belongs to the Suhool tribe (Arabic: السهول), which is an Arab tribe from the Najd region in central Saudi Arabia. The Suhool are documented descendants of the larger Banu Amir tribe, also known as Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, and are considered cousins of the Subay' tribe. The tribe maintained ancestral homes identified by Arab geographers, including Al-Ard, Al-Arid, Al-Uwairid, and Rwaygheb in the Riyadh region, with some branches also found in Kuwait, Iraq, Qatar, and Bahrain. According to Sheikh Muhammad bin Abdullah bin Blaihed, 'The Suhool were penetrating into Najd and settling in Sawad Bahla, known as the width, then they withdrew from it and nothing remained in their hands except Al-Rawdah, known as Rawdah Al-Ard.' Sheikh Saad bin Junaidel noted that 'The Suhool tribe was in control of the country of Ardh Shamam.'

data.originStory: The Al Sahli family traces its origins to the Suhool tribe, which descends from the ancient Arab confederation of Banu Amir ibn Sa'sa'a in the Najd region of central Saudi Arabia.

data.history: The Al Fawaz family name appears in multiple contexts across the Middle East, with notable branches including the Akhras family in Syria and the Al-Hasawi family in Kuwait. The Syrian branch, represented by the Akhras family, originates from Homs and includes Sunni Muslims who established themselves in both Syria and Britain. Fawaz Akhras, born in September 1946 in Homs, qualified as a medical doctor in 1973 and later became a prominent cardiologist in London, founding the British Syrian Society and maintaining significant influence in Syrian-British relations. His daughter Asma married Syrian president Bashar al-Assad in 2000, elevating the family's international profile. The Kuwaiti branch includes Fawaz Mubarak Al-Hasawi, born October 25, 1968, who became a prominent businessman and owned Nottingham Forest Football Club from 2012 to 2017. The family name appears to be distributed across different Gulf and Levantine contexts, though the research does not establish a direct genealogical connection between the various Al Fawaz branches.

data.history: Al Dhafra refers to a geographical region in the western part of Abu Dhabi emirate, historically known as Dhafrah during the Trucial Oman period. The region forms the largest part of Abu Dhabi by area, occupying 71% of the emirate's total territory and stretching across 59,760 square kilometers. Historically, this western region was part of the territory controlled by the Bani Yas confederation, which became the dominant force in most of the area now known as Abu Dhabi by the 18th century. The region includes important oases like Liwa, which has been a permanent settlement area due to adequate underground water sources. The discovery of oil transformed the region's significance, as Al Dhafra's hydrocarbon reserves account for 90% of Abu Dhabi's reserves, which in turn represent 90% of the UAE's total oil and gas resources. Today, the region serves strategic military purposes, housing Al Dhafra Air Base, which has supported coalition operations including the Gulf War, Operation Northern Watch, and operations in the Horn of Africa.
data.description: Al Dhafra is the western region of Abu Dhabi emirate, covering 71% of the emirate's territory and containing 90% of the UAE's oil and gas reserves. The region was historically part of Bani Yas confederation territory and now serves as a strategically important area for both energy resources and military operations.
data.modernStatus: Al Dhafra Region is one of three municipal regions in Abu Dhabi emirate, with its capital at Madinat Zayed. The region is crucial for the UAE's energy sector, containing the country's largest hydrocarbon reserves, and hosts Al Dhafra Air Base, which serves international coalition forces.
data.nameEtymology: Al Dhafra was the historical name of the westernmost region of former Trucial Oman, known as Dhafrah, which was restored as the official name in 2017, replacing the previous designation as Al Gharbia or Western Region.

data.history: Based on the research provided, Al Mansoor appears as a name associated with various individuals across different contexts rather than a single cohesive family lineage. The most prominent historical figure is Abu Mansoor Al-Amriki (Omar Shafik Hammami, 1984-2013), an American citizen of Syrian-Muslim father and Irish-American mother descent who became a leader in the Somali militant group al-Shabaab. His father Shafik Hammami was a Syrian Muslim from Damascus who moved to Alabama for college and became a civil engineer. In Afghanistan, Akhtar Mohammad Mansur (c. 1959-2016) served as the second supreme leader of the Taliban from 2015 until his assassination. He belonged to either the Alizai or Ishaqzai tribes of the Durrani Pashtuns and was born in Kandahar Province. In Bahrain, Mansoor al-Jamri (born 1961) represents a prominent Shia family, being the son of spiritual leader Sheikh Abdul-Amir al-Jamri and serving as editor-in-chief of Al-Wasat newspaper. The research shows these are distinct individuals and families sharing the name Al Mansoor rather than members of a unified Gulf family lineage.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is no direct information about the Al Redha family as a modern GCC genealogical entity. The research focuses entirely on Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (c. 766-818 CE), the eighth Imam in Twelver Shia Islam, who was a direct descendant of the Islamic prophet Muhammad through Ali and Fatima. Al-Rida was born in Medina and lived there until 795/796 CE, when his father Musa al-Kazim was arrested by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid and taken to Iraq. Following his father's death in Baghdad prison in 799/800 CE, al-Rida became his successor according to his father's will, inheriting his property near Medina and the custody of his children and wives. In 816 CE, the Abbasid caliph al-Mamun invited al-Rida to Merv in Khorasan and designated him as heir apparent in 817 CE, though al-Rida accepted reluctantly and stipulated he would not interfere in governmental affairs. Al-Rida died mysteriously in Tus (present-day Mashhad, Iran) in September 818 CE while accompanying al-Mamun back to Baghdad, with many sources suggesting he was poisoned by the caliph. His burial site in Mashhad became the holiest site in Iran for Shia Muslims, with millions making annual pilgrimages to his shrine.
data.description: The research covers Ali ibn Musa al-Rida, the eighth Imam in Twelver Shia Islam who lived from 766-818 CE and was designated heir apparent by the Abbasid caliph al-Mamun. No information is provided about a modern Al Redha family in the GCC region.
data.originStory: According to the research, Ali al-Rida was a descendant of the Islamic prophet Muhammad through his father Musa al-Kazim, the seventh Twelver Shia Imam, who was himself a descendant of Ali and Fatima (cousin and daughter of the Prophet respectively). His mother was reportedly a freed slave of Berber origin named Najma or Tuktam, chosen by Ja'far al-Sadiq.
data.nameEtymology: The title al-Rida (lit. 'the approved one') was reportedly bestowed upon him by the caliph al-Mamun, referencing a semi-messianic descendant of Muhammad upon whom Muslims would agree for the caliphate (al-rida min al Muhammad), which was a rallying cry of the Shia and earlier of the Abbasids against the Umayyads.

data.history: The Al Humoud family appears to be connected to Kuwait's ruling Al Sabah family through marriage ties. The most documented connection is through Shahrazad Al-Humoud Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, who married former Kuwaiti Prime Minister Nasser Al-Mohammed Al-Sabah. This marriage alliance represents the family's integration into Kuwait's political elite structure. The research indicates that Ahmad Humoud Al Sabah served as interior minister, being replaced by Mohammad Al Khalid Al Sabah in October 1996, and later being replaced again by the same individual in December 2013. The family name Al Humoud appears in various governmental positions throughout Kuwait's modern political history, suggesting they have maintained influence in the country's administrative and political structures. However, the research does not provide comprehensive details about the family's origins, tribal affiliations, or early history beyond their documented roles in modern Kuwaiti governance.

data.history: The Al Khudher family name derives from the Arabic word خضر (Khadr/Khidr), meaning 'green,' which also references Al-Khidr ('the green one'), a significant figure in Islamic tradition. The name appears in various romanizations across the Arab world, including Al-Khodr, Al-Kheder, and Al-Khudr, sometimes accompanied by the definite article 'Al-'. The most prominent contemporary member of the Al Khudher family is Humood Othman AlKhudher, born in Kuwait on January 24, 1989, who has achieved international recognition as a nasheed singer and songwriter. Humood spent part of his childhood in England before returning to Kuwait, where he obtained his bachelor's degree in Mass Communication from Kuwait University and later pursued music education in Toronto, Canada, and at Berklee College of Music. His musical career began in 1999 as a backing vocalist for his uncle at local events, and he gained significant attention at age 13 with the duet 'Ummi Filisteen' alongside Mishari Al-Aradah. In 2015, he was signed to Awakening Records and released his debut album 'Aseer Ahsan,' which reached number 10 on the Billboard World Albums Chart, establishing the Al Khudher name in contemporary Islamic music.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family whose most notable contemporary member is nasheed singer Humood Othman AlKhudher, born in 1989, who has achieved international recognition in Islamic music.
data.modernStatus: The family is represented in contemporary Islamic entertainment through Humood AlKhudher's successful music career, including international record deals and chart-topping albums in the nasheed genre.
data.nameEtymology: The surname AlKhudher derives from the Arabic word خضر (Khadr/Khidr) meaning 'green,' which references Al-Khidr ('the green one'), a figure in Islam and related religions.

data.history: The Al Shemali family name appears to be derived from the Arabic word "Shamali" meaning "Northern," as evidenced by the Lebanese town of Burj el-Shamali (Northern Tower) in the Tyre District of South Lebanon. This predominantly Shiite Muslim town has historical connections dating back to medieval times when Crusaders constructed a fortified tower on the hill overlooking Tyre, from which the settlement adapted its name. During Ottoman times in the early 17th century, the Druze leader Fakhreddine II encouraged many Metwali (discriminated Shia Muslims) to settle east of Tyre to secure the Damascus road, establishing the area's predominantly Shiite demographic character. The region became politically significant in the modern era through the work of prominent Shiite clerics and the establishment of the Lebanese Communist Party, with several notable figures bearing the Shemali name rising to leadership positions in Lebanese political movements.
data.description: Al Shemali appears to be a family name associated with the northern regions of Lebanon, particularly the Shiite Muslim communities around Tyre in South Lebanon, with possible connections to Lebanese political and religious leadership.
data.modernStatus: The research shows Al Shemali individuals in prominent Lebanese political positions, including Fuad Shemali as an early leader of the Lebanese Communist Party, and Hanna Gharib (whose current party leader position succeeded previous leaders including Fuad Shemali) representing left-wing political movements in modern Lebanon.

data.history: Al Musalam is documented as one of the prominent merchant families among the Aqilat of Al-Qassim Province in Saudi Arabia during the late 1850s and early 1900s. The Aqilat represented settled tribes and families from Najd, specifically from Al-Qassim, who became renowned as traders across the Arabian Peninsula. Al Musalam, alongside other notable families like Aba Alkhail, Al Rumaih, Al Arfaj, and others, were distinguished merchants who traded primarily in gold, Arabian horses, camels, clothing, and food throughout Kuwait, Iraq, the Levant (modern Jordan and Syria), and other regions. The family was part of a unique merchant class known for their distinctive dress, wearing the Aqal and specific uniforms that set them apart from other people in the region. They gained recognition throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Arab world for their courage, generosity, honesty, and patience during the hardships of long-distance trade journeys, significantly contributing to the economic flourishing of the region. The Aqilat, including Al Musalam, were praised by Saudi leadership, with Crown Prince Sultan bin Abdul Aziz noting they were the first to bring trade to the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and King Abdullah Bin Abdulaziz calling them the best and first ambassadors to the Arabian nations.
data.description: Al Musalam was one of the prominent merchant families among the Aqilat of Al-Qassim Province, renowned for long-distance trading across the Arabian Peninsula during the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
data.originStory: Al Musalam originated as part of the settled tribes and families from Najd, specifically from Al-Qassim Province in Saudi Arabia, becoming prominent merchants among the Aqilat during the late 1850s and early 1900s.
data.modernStatus: Historical merchant family recognized for their role in early Saudi Arabian trade development and economic contribution to the Arabian Peninsula region.

data.history: The Agha family represents a diverse lineage with documented origins tracing to both Pashtun ancestors from Afghanistan and connections to early Indian cinema. According to research on Salma Agha, the surname 'Aagha' derives from her Pashtun ancestors from Afghanistan, with her father Liaqat Gul Agha receiving the honorific title 'Aagha' in Iran where he traded in precious stones and antiques - a title given to businessmen of repute. The family's documented presence spans from Amritsar in Punjab, India to Karachi, Pakistan, with members involved in trade, entertainment, and the arts. The lineage includes connections to prominent figures in Indian and Pakistani cinema, with Rafiq Ghaznavi serving as an actor and musician, and his descendants continuing in the entertainment industry. Through marriage connections, the family became linked to other notable film families including the Khan squash family of Pakistan.

data.history: The research provides information about several notable individuals with the name Mansoor from different backgrounds, but does not contain information about a specific Gulf family called 'Mansoor.' The most prominent Mansoor figure identified is Mansoor al-Jamri, a Bahraini journalist and human rights activist born in 1961 in the village of Bani Jamra, Bahrain. He is the son of Sheikh Abdul-Amir al-Jamri, a Shia spiritual leader who became prominent during the 1990s uprising in Bahrain. The al-Jamri family moved to Iraq when Mansoor was an infant, where his father continued religious studies in Najaf from the 1960s to 1973. Upon returning to Bahrain in 1973, Mansoor al-Jamri eventually became a prominent media figure, founding and editing Al-Wasat newspaper in 2002, which became Bahrain's most popular independent daily. During the 2011 Bahraini uprising, al-Jamri played a significant role as a moderate voice calling for compromise, though his newspaper was temporarily suspended and he was forced to resign before being later reinstated. His family represents the intersection of religious scholarship and modern journalism in Bahrain's political landscape.

data.history:
The research provides information about Princess Shams Pahlavi (1917-1996), who was an Iranian royal and elder sister of Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, the last Shah of Iran. She was born in Tehran on 28 October 1917 as the elder daughter of Reza Shah and his consort Tadj ol-Molouk. During her brother's reign, she served as president of the Red Lion and Sun Society, Iran's Red Cross organization, making it the country's largest charitable organization.

In 1937, Shams married Fereydoun Djam, son of then-prime minister Mahmoud Djam, under strict orders from her father, but the marriage was unhappy and they divorced immediately after Reza Shah's death. Following the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran in 1941, she accompanied her father during his exile to Port Louis, Mauritius, and later Johannesburg, South Africa. She later married Mehrdad Pahlbod and lived in the United States from 1945 to 1947, converting to Catholicism in the 1940s.

After returning to Iran following the 1953 coup that re-established her brother's rule, she maintained a low public profile and focused on managing her inherited fortune and charitable work. In the late 1960s and 1970s, she commissioned notable architectural projects including the Pearl Palace in Mehrshahr near Karaj and Villa Mehrafarin in Chalous, Mazandaran. She left Iran after the 1979 Iranian Revolution, settling in Santa Barbara in 1984, where she died of cancer in 1996.
data.description: Iranian royal family, specifically Princess Shams Pahlavi (1917-1996), elder sister of the last Shah of Iran, known for her charitable work and exile following the Iranian Revolution.
data.originStory: Princess Shams was born into the Pahlavi dynasty, established when Reza Shah came to power in Iran. She was born in Tehran on 28 October 1917 as the elder daughter of Reza Shah and his consort Tadj ol-Molouk.
data.modernStatus: The Pahlavi royal family was deposed in 1979 during the Iranian Revolution. Princess Shams died in exile in Santa Barbara, California in 1996.

data.history: The Yasin family name is of Arabic origin, derived from the 36th chapter (surah) of the Quran called Ya-Sin. The research reveals several distinct and unrelated Yasin family lineages across different regions and time periods. In Iraq, Abdul Rahman Yasin was born in Bloomington, Indiana in 1960 to an Iraqi father who was studying for a PhD, with the family subsequently returning to Iraq. Ahmed Yasin, the Hamas co-founder, was born in al-Jura village near Ashkelon in Mandatory Palestine in 1936/1937, with his family fleeing to Gaza during the 1948 Arab-Israeli War. The footballer Ahmed Yasin represents a different lineage - a Feyli Kurdish family that lived on Falastin Street in Baghdad before emigrating to Sweden in the 1990s due to the Iraq-Iran War and subsequent conflicts. In Kashmir, Yasin Malik was born in Srinagar in 1966 and became a prominent separatist leader. These examples demonstrate that Yasin families have diverse ethnic and geographic origins, united primarily by their adoption of this religiously significant Arabic name rather than by common ancestry.
data.description: Yasin is an Arabic surname and given name derived from the Quranic chapter Ya-Sin, adopted by families of diverse ethnic backgrounds across the Middle East, South Asia, and diaspora communities worldwide.
data.originStory: The name Yasin originates from the 36th chapter (surah) of the Quran called Ya-Sin, making it a religiously significant name adopted by Muslim families across different ethnic and geographic backgrounds rather than indicating a single family lineage.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Yasin families are found globally in various professions and political roles, including political leadership, sports, academia, and business, with no unified modern organizational structure.

data.history: The Al Nuaymi family are the ruling family of the Emirate of Ajman in the United Arab Emirates, belonging to the Al Na'im tribe. Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III has served as the 10th ruler of Ajman since September 6, 1981, when he succeeded his father Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III. He had previously served as deputy ruler since 1960. The family's political prominence extends beyond Ajman, with connections to other UAE ruling families through marriage - Sheikh Humaid's sister Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi was the mother of Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi, the current ruler of Fujairah. The family has maintained significant political influence in the UAE federal structure, with Sheikh Humaid serving as a member of the Federal Supreme Council. The Al Nuaymi lineage has also produced other notable political figures, including Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi, who served as foreign minister of the UAE and in various senior diplomatic positions from the 1970s through 1990.
data.description: The Al Nuaymi family are the ruling dynasty of Ajman emirate in the UAE, belonging to the Al Na'im tribe and maintaining significant political influence at both emirate and federal levels since the formation of the UAE in 1971.

data.history: The Al Sabah family, also known as the House of Al-Sabah, is the ruling family of Kuwait and originates from the Bani Utbah confederation. According to oral traditions, they were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their predatory habits of preying on caravans in Basra and trading ships in the Shatt al-Arab. One tradition states they fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then to Zubara on Qatar's west coast, before finally settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s where they found water. The name 'Bani Utub' allegedly derives from their final northward migration (atabu-ila al-shimal). Soon after founding their settlement in Kuwait, a Sabah became leader, ruling until his death in 1762. The family has ruled Kuwait continuously since then, with the critical alliance with Britain forged during Mubarak the Great's reign (1896-1915), Kuwait's independence achieved under Sheikh Abdullah Al-Salim in 1961, and the family maintaining power through the Gulf War and into the modern era.
data.description: The Al Sabah family is the ruling dynasty of Kuwait, originating from the Bani Utbah confederation and settling in Kuwait around the mid-1700s. They have ruled Kuwait continuously since 1752, currently headed by Emir Sheikh Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah.
data.originStory: The Al Sabah family originate from the Bani Utbah confederation. According to one oral tradition, they were expelled from Umm Qasr in southern Iraq by the Ottomans due to their predatory habits. According to another tradition told to the Political Agent by Shaikh Abdulla, the Sabahs fled drought in central Arabia in the early 1700s, migrated south, then to Zubara on Qatar's west coast, before finally settling in Kuwait where they found water.
data.modernStatus: Currently the ruling family of Kuwait, holding the positions of Emir and key government ministries including defense, interior, and foreign affairs. The family has faced recent internal political feuds involving corruption allegations and has consolidated power through constitutional suspensions and citizenship revocation campaigns.

folkLegends.title: Origin of Bani Utub Name
folkLegends.story: On the last leg of their journey to Kuwait, the Al Sabah family 'atabu-ila al-shimal' (moved to the north), which according to tradition was the origin of the name Bani Utub

data.nameEtymology: Al-Sabah means 'the morning' in Arabic. The family name derives from their founding patriarch Sabah I bin Jaber who ruled from 1752-1762.

migrationPath.description: Migration from drought-stricken central Arabia or expulsion from Umm Qasr by Ottomans
Final settlement in Kuwait where they found water and established their rule

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Sabah I bin Jaber
notableFigures.title: 1st Ruler of Kuwait
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founding patriarch and first ruler of Kuwait
notableFigures.biography: First ruler of Kuwait from 1752-1762, established Al Sabah dynasty rule. Became leader soon after the family founded their settlement in Kuwait.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Mubarak Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: 7th Ruler of Kuwait, Mubarak the Great
notableFigures.roleDescription: Forged critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain
notableFigures.biography: Ruled 1896-1915 after killing his brothers Mohammed and Jarrah in 1896. Forged the critical alliance between Kuwait and Britain that shaped Kuwait's modern history.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Abdullah Al-Salim Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: 11th Ruler/1st Emir of Kuwait
notableFigures.roleDescription: Ended British protectorate and established modern Kuwaiti state
notableFigures.biography: Ruled 1950-1965, ended British protectorate status by signing treaty on June 19, 1961. Introduced Kuwait's Constitution in 1962 and established Parliament in 1963.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmed Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: Sports Administrator and Military Officer
notableFigures.roleDescription: Founded Asian Handball Federation and Kuwait Olympic Committee, killed defending Kuwait
notableFigures.biography: Son of Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, served as military officer and sports administrator. Founded Asian Handball Federation and served as IOC member 1981-1990, killed by Iraqi forces at Dasman Palace on first day of invasion.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Jaber Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: 13th Ruler/3rd Emir of Kuwait
notableFigures.roleDescription: Led Kuwait through Gulf War era
notableFigures.biography: Ruled 1977-2006, led Kuwait through Iran-Iraq War and Gulf War. Established government in exile in Saudi Arabia during Iraqi occupation and returned to Kuwait in March 1991.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: 15th Ruler/5th Emir of Kuwait
notableFigures.roleDescription: Longest-serving foreign minister turned Emir, regional mediator
notableFigures.biography: Served as foreign minister 1963-2003 (longest-serving globally at time), then Emir 2006-2020. Known for humanitarian leadership and regional mediation efforts, particularly in Qatar diplomatic crisis.

notableFigures.name: Sheikh Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah
notableFigures.title: 17th Ruler/7th Emir of Kuwait
notableFigures.roleDescription: Current Emir, former intelligence chief
notableFigures.biography: Current Emir since 2023, spent career in security and intelligence. Served as head of state security 1967-1980, deputy chief of Kuwait National Guard 2004-2020, became Crown Prince in 2020.

data.history: The Al Hadidi (الحديدي) is an Arab tribe primarily located in Syria. According to available research, the tribe is of Muslim origins and claims descent from the Prophet Mohammad. The family has demonstrated significant influence on cultural and social movements in their communities, with members being highly educated and contributing to various professional fields. The Al-Hadidi name appears in historical records as being related to the Al-Musawi family lineage, which traces its ancestry through the Twelve Imams of Shia Islam. However, the specific historical details of the Al Hadidi family's migration patterns, founding events, or detailed genealogical connections beyond this general association remain undocumented in the available research materials.
data.description: Al-Hadidi is an Arab tribe primarily based in Syria, known for being highly educated with many scholars and physicians, and claiming descent from Prophet Mohammad.
data.originStory: The tribe claims descent from the Prophet Mohammad and is noted as being of Muslim origins, though specific genealogical details are not provided in the research.
data.modernStatus: The family has produced numerous scholars, including published PhD holders, UIUX designers, and particularly physicians who practice medicine, demonstrating significant influence in cultural and social movements.

connections.context: Al-Hadidi is listed among the Al-Musawi related families in the genealogical records

data.history: The Al Ghaithi family name appears prominently in modern Libyan political administration, specifically in Derna. Abdulmenam Al-Ghaithi served as mayor of Derna during the catastrophic dam collapse disaster of September 2023 caused by Storm Daniel. Following the collapse of the Derna and Abu Mansour dams, which released approximately 30 million cubic meters of water and devastated the city, Al-Ghaithi made controversial statements about the potential death toll, telling al-Arabiya that it could range from 18,000 to 20,000 people - equivalent to a fifth of Derna's population. His handling of the crisis became a focal point of public anger and political accountability. On 18 September 2023, protesters burned down Al-Ghaithi's residence as part of demonstrations against local officials' failure to maintain the dams or issue evacuation orders. He was subsequently suspended from his mayoral position on 25 September 2023 and detained along with several other officials on charges of mismanagement and negligence. By July 2024, he was among 12 officials who received jail sentences for their role in the dam disaster.
data.description: Al Ghaithi is a family name associated with political leadership in Derna, Libya, most notably through Abdulmenam Al-Ghaithi who served as mayor during the 2023 dam disaster.
data.modernStatus: The family gained prominence through municipal political leadership in Derna, Libya, though this was severely damaged by the 2023 dam collapse crisis and subsequent legal proceedings.

notableFigures.name: Abdulmenam Al-Ghaithi
notableFigures.title: Mayor of Derna
notableFigures.roleDescription: Served as mayor of Derna during the 2023 dam collapse disaster, later convicted for mismanagement
